failure_surface: Formula uses full-column references ($B:$B) instead of bounded data ranges, which may include empty rows beyond actual dataset and cause incorrect filtering results or performance degradation
root_cause: The agent generated formulas using unrestricted column references like ROW($B:$B) and $B:$B without determining actual data boundaries, which can include header rows or excessive empty rows that interfere with array calculations like AGGREGATE
minimal_fix_hypothesis: Before writing formulas that iterate or filter across ranges, dynamically determine the actual data end row by checking for non-empty cells, then construct bounded range references (e.g., $B$2:$B$lastRow) instead of full-column references
evidence: Agent discovered max_row=100 in initial exploration but still used $B:$B (full column) in final formula
First column headers show Type at row 1, meaning header row is included in $B:$B which may affect >0 comparison
AGGREGATE with full column references processes ~1M rows unnecessarily and may include unintended blank/header rows

memory_items.title: Use Bounded Ranges in Array Formulas
memory_items.lesson: When constructing formulas with iterative/aggregation functions like AGGREGATE, FILTER, or INDEX+MATCH combinations, always determine the actual data end row programmatically before generating the formula string.
memory_items.generalization: Full-column references ($X:$X) should be avoided in formulas that perform array calculations. Use determined bounds like $X$2:$X${lastRow} to ensure correct filtering, avoid header inclusion, and improve performance.

memory_items.title: Verify Data Bounds Before Formula Generation
memory_items.lesson: After inspecting a worksheet, explicitly compute the last populated row for relevant columns and incorporate that into formula templates before writing them.
memory_items.generalization: Pattern: query max_row for data columns → substitute into formula string → write formula. Do not hardcode large arbitrary ranges or use full-column notation unless explicitly required.

memory_items.title: Handle Header Row Separately in Filtered Queries
memory_items.lesson: When filtering data columns, exclude the header row from range calculations to prevent false positives or misaligned results in aggregate operations.
memory_items.generalization: For filtered list generation formulas, start ranges from row 2 (data row) and calculate bounds based on actual content, ensuring headers are never evaluated against filter conditions like >0.

edits.content:
- **Range Bounds**: When creating formulas that filter, aggregate, or iterate (e.g., AGGREGATE, FILTER, INDEX+MATCH), programmatically determine actual data end rows and use bounded ranges ($A$2:$A$lastRow) instead of full-column references ($A:$A). Always exclude header rows from filter conditions.
- **Array Formula Compatibility**: Test array formulas on known small ranges first. Avoid full-column references in AGGREGATE or similar functions as they may include unexpected blank/header rows and degrade performance.
